# Diffusion Models {.section-divider}

## Core Intuition

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
- Start from structured data.
- Gradually add noise until the data is nearly random.
- Learn to reverse this process by denoising.
:::

::: {.figure-placeholder}
<div class="placeholder-title">Noising and denoising process</div>
<div class="placeholder-path">figures/diffusion_process.png</div>
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: introduce diffusion models through the noising-and-denoising story before showing equations.

Intuition: the model learns many small cleanup steps, like gradually sharpening a corrupted signal.

Warning: the noise process is a training construction; the robot is not physically adding noise to its actions during deployment.
:::

---

## From Images to Actions

Diffusion models are widely known for image generation, but the same idea can generate:

- robot joint commands
- end-effector poses
- action trajectories
- control parameters

The sample space changes; the denoising principle remains.

::: {.notes}
Main point: make clear that diffusion is a general generative modeling recipe, not only an image model.

Intuition: actions can be treated as structured vectors or sequences, just as images are structured arrays.

Warning: action generation has stricter timing and safety requirements than offline image generation.
:::

---

## Forward Noising Process

A common forward process is:

$$
q(x_k \mid x_{k-1})
=
\mathcal{N}
\left(
x_k;
\sqrt{1-\beta_k}x_{k-1},
\beta_k I
\right)
$$

where $k$ indexes diffusion time, not robot time.

::: {.notes}
Main point: explain the forward noising equation and the meaning of the diffusion index.

Intuition: each step slightly shrinks the clean signal and adds Gaussian noise until structure disappears.

Warning: emphasize that $k$ is diffusion time, not the robot time $t$ used for control trajectories.
:::

---

## Reverse Denoising Process

The learned model predicts how to move from noisy samples toward clean data:

$$
p_\theta(x_{k-1} \mid x_k, c)
$$

where $c$ is optional conditioning information such as images, proprioception, or task context.

::: {.notes}
Main point: the reverse process is the learned generative direction, conditioned on the task context.

Intuition: conditioning tells the denoiser what kind of sample should emerge from noise.

Warning: do not describe the model as exactly inverting the forward process; it learns an approximate reverse distribution from data.
:::

---

## Noise Prediction Objective

A standard training objective predicts the injected noise:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{diff}(\theta)
=
\mathbb{E}_{x_0,\epsilon,k}
\left[
\lVert
\epsilon - \epsilon_\theta(x_k, k, c)
\rVert^2
\right]
$$

::: {.notes}
Main point: the training target is often the noise that was added, not the clean sample directly.

Intuition: predicting noise gives the model a consistent denoising task across many corruption levels.

Warning: this is one common parameterization; papers may also predict clean samples or velocity-like variables.
:::

---

## Sampling Bottleneck

- Sampling requires repeated denoising steps.
- More steps usually improve sample quality.
- Robot control also requires bounded latency.

This tradeoff becomes central when diffusion models are used as policies.

::: {.notes}
Main point: connect diffusion sampling cost to real-time robot control constraints.

Intuition: an image generator can spend seconds sampling; a controller may have only milliseconds before the next command is due.

Warning: fewer steps are not automatically better if action quality or stability degrades.
:::

---

## DDPM vs DDIM

- DDPM: stochastic denoising sampler.
- DDIM: deterministic or partially deterministic sampler with fewer steps.

::: {.figure-placeholder}
<div class="placeholder-title">DDPM vs DDIM comparison table</div>
<div class="placeholder-path">figures/ddpm_ddim_comparison.png</div>
:::

::: {.notes}
Main point: introduce DDIM as one route toward faster diffusion-policy inference.

Intuition: DDPM is closer to a slow random walk through denoising; DDIM can take more direct deterministic steps.

Warning: avoid overclaiming that DDIM always preserves performance; the speed-quality tradeoff is empirical.
:::
